behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: The core risk is not the grouping algorithm itself, but how trace IDs are extracted and grouped across **multiple real log sources**. A unit test with mocked inputs would only test the grouping logic on in-memory objects. Real failures occur when: logs come from actual files/streams, have format variations across services, or arrive out-of-order. The grouping must work on logs ingested from multiple sources simultaneously, which requires testing file I/O or multi-source streaming.
behaviors.test_description: Load logs from multiple simulated microservice sources (e.g., separate files or mock streams), verify that entries with identical trace IDs are grouped together, and confirm grouping is unaffected by which service emitted each entry. Test with realistic log formats and timing variations.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test correctly requires Integration level — simulating multiple log sources and verifying grouping across them. Unit test would be too isolated.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Clock skew handling is not just about sorting — it requires detecting and/or adjusting for timing differences between independent sources. A unit test with pre-ordered timestamps would only verify the sort function, not whether the system correctly handles real clock skew scenarios. Real failure: two services emit logs at nearly the same logical instant, but their clocks differ by 500ms. Testing this requires simulating multiple log sources with **actual clock offsets** and verifying the timeline remains correct, which is inherently a multi-source integration concern.
behaviors.test_description: Create logs from simulated microservices with deliberately skewed timestamps (e.g., service A at T, service B at T+450ms for the same event). Verify the timeline view orders them correctly, respecting any clock skew detection or adjustment mechanism. Test with edge cases at ±500ms boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test correctly requires Integration level to simulate multiple time-shifted sources. A unit test of the sort function alone would miss skew-handling logic.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Spike detection depends on the **full pipeline**: ingest logs → extract latencies → compute baseline → detect spike. A unit test of spike-detection math alone (e.g., `if p99_current > 3*baseline`) would miss real failures in latency extraction from logs or baseline calculation. The baseline must be computed from actual log data (either a training set or rolling window), and latencies must be correctly extracted from log entries. A failure in any of these steps breaks spike detection. Testing requires integrating log ingestion with latency analysis.
behaviors.test_description: Ingest a set of baseline logs with normal latencies, compute baseline p99. Then ingest a second batch with latencies >3x the baseline. Verify the spike is detected and flagged in the anomaly report. Include edge cases where p99 is exactly 3x and slightly below 3x.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test correctly requires Integration level — from log ingestion through spike detection. A unit test of the threshold comparison would pass even if baseline calculation or latency extraction were broken.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily a **localized error-handling test** for the JSON parsing function. The real failure is: malformed JSON causes a parse exception that is either uncaught (crashes) or not counted. The fix is straightforward: catch JSON parse exceptions, increment a skip counter, and continue. A unit test can verify this with actual malformed JSON strings (e.g., truncated JSON, invalid syntax) without needing a full pipeline. The skip counter is a simple side effect that can be verified in the same test.
behaviors.test_description: Call the JSON parsing function with three categories of input: valid JSON (verify parsed), malformed JSON (verify skipped, count incremented), and partially-malformed JSON. Confirm the function returns parsed entries and a correct skip count without raising unhandled exceptions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test correctly operates at Unit level — testing the error handler in isolation. No need for full pipeline or file I/O.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: This is a **real-world performance test** that cannot be accurately validated with in-memory mocks. The failure is observable only under sustained load: dropped entries, lag/backlog, memory exhaustion, or thread saturation. A mock that provides 10,000 objects instantly from memory would not reveal I/O bottlenecks, thread contention, or backpressure failures. Testing requires simulating or generating **real-time streaming** (file, network, or in-process stream) at the target rate, with real resource constraints and performance measurement. The success criterion is measurable: all entries processed, latency stable, no drops.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or simulate 10,000 log entries per second (via file, network stream, or in-process generator) for a sustained period (e.g., 10+ seconds). Measure throughput and verify: (1) all entries are processed (no drops detected in output), (2) no backlog/lag accumulates, (3) resource usage remains stable. Use realistic log sizes and network latency if applicable.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test correctly requires System level — real-time streaming, I/O characteristics, and performance measurement. An Integration test with a large batch would not reveal streaming/timing issues.
